scenarios.task: Vendor sent us their SaaS MSA. The limitation of liability says: 'Neither party's aggregate liability shall exceed the fees paid in the twelve (12) months preceding the claim. Neither party shall be liable for any indirect, incidental, or consequential damages. The foregoing limitations shall not apply to Customer's breach of Sections 4 (Payment) or 9 (Confidentiality).' We're the customer, handling PII in their system. Review this clause.
criteria: Spots that the carve-outs are one-way: only the customer's likely breaches (payment, confidentiality) are uncapped while the vendor's likely failure modes stay capped
Flags the absence of a data-breach/data-protection carve-out or super-cap given PII is being processed, and proposes one (e.g. 2-3x fees)
Notes the consequential-damages waiver interacts with the cap to bar the damages a data incident would actually cause
Proposes concrete redline language or fallback positions, not just 'this is unfavorable'
Reads the clause from the failure scenario (what happens when things go wrong), not the happy path

scenarios.task: We're signing a $9k/year tool subscription and separately a $400k/year data processing agreement with a new vendor this month. Our legal budget is tight. How much review effort should each get, and what would you actually look at in each?
criteria: Explicitly tiers review depth by contract value and data sensitivity rather than applying uniform diligence
For the small subscription: names a minimal targeted check (auto-renewal window, liability basics, data terms) instead of a full markup
For the $400k data agreement: prescribes full review naming specific high-stakes clauses (LoL, indemnity, data protection, termination/data return)
Mentions the auto-renewal notice window as a trap worth diarying even on small contracts
Treats over-lawyering the small contract as a real cost, not free safety

scenarios.task: Our MSA says the order form controls over the MSA on conflict, and the vendor's order form incorporates their 'Service Terms available at [URL] as updated from time to time.' Procurement says this is standard boilerplate and wants to sign today. Anything to worry about?
criteria: Identifies that URL terms 'as updated from time to time' let the vendor unilaterally amend the deal after signature
Traces the order-of-precedence chain to show the URL terms may effectively sit above the negotiated MSA
Recommends freezing the URL terms (attach a dated copy as an exhibit) or requiring notice/consent for changes
Pushes back on 'standard boilerplate' as a reason to accept, while keeping the response proportionate
Recommends archiving a dated copy of the current URL terms regardless of the negotiation outcome
